title: Process for the recovery of value metals from zinc-bearing ores, concentrates, intermediates and wastes
abstract: A process for recovering zinc and associated value metals from zinc-bearing ores, concentrates and intermediates, especially from electric arc furnace dust (EAFD) and spent steel mill zinc pickle liquor (ZPL), comprising leaching the feed material in recycled, concentrated hydrochloric acid, recovering iron as a pure form of hematite, precipitating pure zinc hydroxide and drying said product to form pure zinc oxide, and recovering magnesia via pyrohydrolysis. Value metals such as silver, copper, lead and cadmium are also recovered as pure products. A particular embodiment is to combine EAFD with spent steel plant pickling liquors, and in particular with spent steel plant ZPL. The present invention provides a single process which permits the recovery of iron, zinc and any other metals in a form of economic value, and to generate within the process its own reagents for internal recycle.
claims:
Claims Claims 1. A process for recovering zinc and associated value metals from zinc-bearing feed material, the process comprising: a) providing a feed material comprising at least zinc and iron; b) leaching the feed material with a HC1 leaching medium to generate a leachate and a solid residue, the leachate comprising at least zinc and iron; c) separating the solid residue from the leachate to generate a solution containing iron; d) heating the solution containing iron to cause precipitation of the metals, except zinc or calcium and any alkali metals if present, to generate a heated slurry and to recover hydrochloric acid in a concentrated form; e) quenching the heated slurry in water and fdtering off resulting solids and a fdtrate; and f) treating the fdtrate from quenching to recover zinc hydroxide. 2. The process of claim 1, further comprising oxidising the solution containing iron to convert ferrous iron to ferric iron, the iron in the solution being predominantly in its ferric state or brought to its ferric state. 3. The process of claim 2, wherein the oxidation of ferrous iron is carried out electrolytically or by adding HC1. 4. The process of any one of claims 1 to 3, wherein the feed material comprises other metals selected from the group consisting of copper, silver, lead, cadmium, manganese and magnesium, the method further comprising leaching the resulting solids in dilute hydrochloric acid to effect re-dissolution of at least one of copper, silver, lead, cadmium, manganese and magnesium if present. 5. The process of claim 4, wherein, the amount of hydrochloric acid as used is at least stoichiometric for the other metals present. 6. The process of any one of claims 1 to 5, further comprising treating the fdtrate from zinc precipitation using pyrohydrolysis to recover HC1. 7. The process of claim 6, wherein a part of HC1 recovered by pyrohydrolysis is recycled 

 within the process. 8. The process of any one of claims 1 to 7, wherein the feed material is electric arc furnace dust (EAFD), or a complex lead-zinc-copper-silver ore or concentrate. 9. The process of claim 8, wherein spent zinc pickle liquor is added to the electric arc furnace dust to provide additional iron and a portion of the HC1 leaching medium. 10. The process of any one of claims 1 to 9, wherein the leaching step is performed at a temperature of 25-110Â°C, preferably 80-100Â°C, more preferably 90-95Â°C. 11. The process of any one of claims 1 to 10, wherein the leachate is cooled to crystallise out lead chloride. 12. The process of any one of claims 1 to 11, wherein in step (d) the ferric solution is heated to a temperature of about 170-200Â°C, preferably about 180-190Â°C, in order to effect the hydrolysis of ferric iron and precipitation of hematite. 13. The process of claim 12, wherein the reaction in step (d) is allowed to proceed until no more HC1 is evolved. 14. The process of claim 12, wherein water or steam is added at the end of the reaction in step (d) to ensure complete hydrolysis of all metals present except zinc, calcium and the alkali metals which do not hydrolyse. 15. The process of any one of claims 1 to 14, wherein the amount of water used in the quenching step (e) is controlled to produce a magnesium chloride solution of 30 wt.% to 50 wt.%, when magnesium is present in the feed material.
